name: master mint cilantro skillet
instructions:
THREE VARIANTS. All share the mint + cilantro herb pair and indian-spice register, but the structural patterns are different — two are mortar-pestle herb-sauce-on-protein (priya krishna), one is a tarka-style dal (madhur jaffrey).

VARIANT A — CHICKEN (priya krishna):
- Cook chicken breast in turmeric, chili powder, cumin, coriander.
- Mash cilantro, mint, garlic, ginger, and lime in a mortar into a rough paste.
- Add the mash to the cooked chicken at the END — do not cook it in.

VARIANT B — TOFU:
- Use firm tofu, sliced flat and large. Boil or press first.
- Dredge slices in starch (potato or corn) and pan-cook with turmeric, chili powder, cumin, coriander.
- Same mortar-pestle finish as the chicken variant: mash cilantro, mint, garlic, ginger, lime; add at the end, do not cook in.

VARIANT C — LENTILS (madhur jaffrey, tarka-style dal):
- Bloom whole spices in 1 tbsp vegetable oil: 1/2 tsp cumin seeds, 1/2 tsp mustard seeds, a pinch of asafetida, 1-3 whole dried red chiles.
- Add 1 small yellow onion in fine half rings, sauté briefly. Add 2 cloves garlic chopped, then 1 medium tomato chopped.
- Add 1 cup whole green lentils, water to cover, 3/4 tsp salt, 1 tsp ground coriander. Simmer until lentils are tender.
- Finish with 1/2 cup chopped fresh cilantro and 1/2 cup chopped fresh mint stirred in off-heat.
notes:
TWO STRUCTURAL PATTERNS UNDER ONE HERB PAIR:
- Chicken and tofu (priya krishna pattern): mortar-pestle herb sauce on a spice-cooked protein. The mash goes on at the END, never cooked in.
- Lentils (madhur jaffrey pattern): tarka-style dal with whole spices bloomed in oil, onion/tomato base, herbs stirred in off-heat at the finish.
The herb pair (mint + cilantro) is the through-line; everything else is variant-specific.

DO NOT COOK THE HERBS IN — VARIANTS A AND B: this is the key technique for the chicken and tofu variants. Treating these like a mole and cooking the herb mash into a sauce makes the herbs lose flavor and turns the oil an unappetizing green. The mash is applied at the end, off heat or just barely warm. The mortar-pestle release of oils is what carries flavor — not extended cooking.

LENTILS USE A DIFFERENT TECHNIQUE — STIRRED IN AT THE END: variant C has the mint and cilantro stirred in raw at the end of the dal cook, off heat. Same don't-cook-the-herbs principle, different application — no mortar work, just chopped and folded in.

VARIANT A — CHICKEN BREAST OVER THIGH: priya krishna's recipe works better with chicken breast than thigh. Breast carries the spice-and-herb register cleanly; thigh's fattiness competes with the bright herb mash.

VARIANT B — FIRM TOFU, SLICED LARGE AND FLAT, BOILED/PRESSED: tofu variant only works with firm tofu, sliced flat and large (not cubed), boiled or pressed first to drive out water. Cubes don't carry the herb mash the same way; soft tofu disintegrates in the pan.

VARIANT C — TOMATO AND ASAFETIDA ARE THE LENTIL-SPECIFIC INGREDIENTS: unlike the chicken and tofu variants, the lentil variant uses tomato (one medium, chopped, into the tarka), asafetida (a pinch with the whole spices), mustard seeds, dried red chiles, and yellow onion. These are absent from variants A and B — they belong to the dal pattern, not the mortar-pestle-herb-sauce pattern. Don't backport them to the chicken or tofu variants.

NO GINGER, LIME, TURMERIC, OR CHILI POWDER IN VARIANT C: the dal variant skips ginger and lime (the mortar-pestle brightness slot is handled differently here) and uses dried red chiles + asafetida instead of turmeric and chili powder. The two patterns have separate spice axes.

OIL RESTRAINT IN VARIANT C: madhur jaffrey's recipe calls for 3 tbsp vegetable oil; 1 tbsp is enough. Don't over-oil the tarka.

MINT CILANTRO CHIMICHURRI IS A NATURAL PAIRING FOR VARIANTS A AND B: the mint cilantro chimichurri recipe (variant A — indian, with lime and ginger) is a great condiment alongside either the chicken or the tofu variant here. Both variants already lean on the mortar-pestle mint-cilantro mash as the finish; the chimichurri extends that flavor axis with a raw allium + vinegar structure that adds acid, crunch, and a chutney-adjacent register. Spoon over the plated protein at the table. Not appropriate for variant C — the tarka-style dal has its own herbal finish and the chimichurri's vinegar would fight the tomato and asafetida base.
